coredata.dc:description: Phenylenediamine has become a classic material for synthesizing carbon dots (CDs) due to its nitrogen doping, conjugated aromatic structure, low cost, and easy availability. Typically, the CDs obtained from m-phenylenediamine (mPD), o-phenylenediamine (oPD) and p-phenylenediamine (pPD) exhibit fluorescence that follows a rule from blue to red. However, their photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) is low, and the spectral coverage is relatively small, which greatly limits their application and development in high-quality white LEDs. Herein, we selected a simple and environmentally friendly succinic acid (SA) to regulate the luminescence properties, which achieve unconventional luminescent behaviors with high PLQY values (68 %, 60 %, and 72 %) and wide spectral coverage. The PL emission of the obtained CDs followed the rule of pPD, mPD, and oPD from deep-blue to red, which is different from most previously reported results. Additionally, The SA molecules altered the polymerization structure and led to changes in the conjugated domain and surface C=O and –COOH groups, achieving this unconventional luminescence with high PLQY values. Finally, high-quality white LEDs were fabricated with the CIE coordinates of (0.34, 0.33), close to the (0.33, 0.33) of pure white light. R-CDs with an absolute PLQY value of up to 72 % were also used in bioimaging. We use the same small organic acid to regulate the phenylenediamine isomer and obtain CDs with fluorescence in different shift directions. This work will provide a new idea for luminescence regulation with high PLQY values and wide spectral coverage.

head.ce:title: Regulation of the unconventional luminescence behaviors of phenylenediamine-based carbon dots with high PLQY values

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 211189 China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 211189, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Succinic acid regulates phenylenediamine isomers to achieve unconventional luminescent behaviors carbon dots with high PLQY values (68%, 60%, and 72%) and wide spectral coverage, and their applications in high-quality multicolor/white LEDs and live cell imaging.

Phenylenediamine has become a classic material for synthesizing carbon dots (CDs) due to its nitrogen doping, conjugated aromatic structure, low cost, and easy availability. Typically, the CDs obtained from m-phenylenediamine (mPD), o-phenylenediamine (oPD) and p-phenylenediamine (pPD) exhibit fluorescence that follows a rule from blue to red. However, their photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) is low, and the spectral coverage is relatively small, which greatly limits their application and development in high-quality white LEDs. Herein, we selected a simple and environmentally friendly succinic acid (SA) to regulate the luminescence properties, which achieve unconventional luminescent behaviors with high PLQY values (68 %, 60 %, and 72 %) and wide spectral coverage. The PL emission of the obtained CDs followed the rule of pPD, mPD, and oPD from deep-blue to red, which is different from most previously reported results. Additionally, The SA molecules altered the polymerization structure and led to changes in the conjugated domain and surface C=O and –COOH groups, achieving this unconventional luminescence with high PLQY values. Finally, high-quality white LEDs were fabricated with the CIE coordinates of (0.34, 0.33), close to the (0.33, 0.33) of pure white light. R-CDs with an absolute PLQY value of up to 72 % were also used in bioimaging. We use the same small organic acid to regulate the phenylenediamine isomer and obtain CDs with fluorescence in different shift directions. This work will provide a new idea for luminescence regulation with high PLQY values and wide spectral coverage.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of environmentally friendly fluorescent nanoparticles, have a sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon structure in the carbon core and are bonded with different types of chemical groups on the surface. They have a series of advantages, such as small particle size (usually less than 10 nm), high chemical stability, tunable luminescence wavelength, good biocompatibility, low cost, and easy availability of raw materials [1–3], making them a rising star among many luminescent materials. In recent years, with the efforts and exploration of scientists, CDs have seen significant applications and developments in optoelectronic devices [4,5], biomedicine [6,7], ion detection [8,9], solar concentrators [10,11], catalysis [12,13], and anti-counterfeiting [14,15].

Currently, with the increasing demand for full-color lighting and display devices, many studies are focusing on the luminescence regulation of CDs. Researchers are striving to find a simple and effective method to obtain full-color CDs with high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) values. Phenylenediamine has become a widely used raw material among researchers due to its advantages, such as nitrogen doping, high conjugation, low cost, and easy availability. It has three isomers, namely o-phenylenediamine (oPD), m-phenylenediamine (mPD), and p-phenylenediamine (pPD). Due to the different steric hindrance effects of the amino functional groups at various positions, there are differences in the reactivity of the amino groups. This results in the formation of CDs with different conjugated structures and fluorescent emissions during the polymerization and carbonization processes. In general, mPD as a single precursor can produce blue CDs, oPD as a single precursor can get green or yellow CDs, and pPD as a single precursor can obtain red CDs. For example, Jiang et al. [16] used mPD, oPD, and pPD as precursors, with anhydrous ethanol as the solvent, to obtain blue (435 nm), green (535 nm), and red CDs (604 nm) through a solvothermal reaction process. The corresponding PLQY values were 4.8 %, 17.6 %, and 26.1 %, respectively. The variations in the emission spectra of these CDs may be attributed to differences in particle size and nitrogen content. Chao et al. [17] used oPD as the carbon source and deionized water as the solvent to obtain yellow CDs via the hydrothermal method. Wang et al. [18] obtained red CDs by rapidly carbonizing pPD dissolved in a mixed ethanol and H2O solution using microwave irradiation. The fluorescence emission peak was at 615 nm, with a PLQY value of 15 %. Sun et al. [19] developed a simple solvent-free method to obtain red CDs with the same emission wavelength of 615 nm by heating pPD powder in an autoclave at 200 ℃. The above work all confirms that the fluorescence emission of phenylenediamine-based CDs follows the rule of blue to red from mPD, oPD to pPD, which is considered the conventional luminescence behavior. However, the relatively low PLQY value and narrow spectral coverage are not conducive to achieving high-quality white light devices. To better meet the urgent needs in the fields of multicolor/white lighting and display, intensive research on their luminescence regulation is being carried out. Cheng et al. [20] used mPD as a precursor, anhydrous ethanol as a solvent, and dilute sulfuric acid as an additive to achieve a shift in the fluorescence emission of CDs from blue to green. The obtained green CDs can be effectively applied in the field of backlight displays with an improved PLQY value of about 80 %. Our group [21] used oPD as a precursor and introduced three different metal cation catalysts (AlCl3, FeCl3, and CuCl2), which prompted the oPD molecules to form conjugated structure complexes and accelerated the dehydrogenation and polymerization processes. This increased the sp2-conjugated domains and graphite N content, effectively achieving the conversion of CDs from yellow to red. The catalytic effect of the AlCl3 increases the PLQY value from 11.94 % to 29.99 %. Xu et al. [22] similarly used oPD as a raw material, introducing glacial acetic acid to promote the proportion of sp3 bonding, which significantly reduced the size of sp2-conjugated units in the CDs, shifting the PL emission to the ultraviolet B region. Ding et al. [23] used pPD as the carbon source and urea as the nitrogen doping agent to obtain mixed CDs by a one-step hydrothermal method, and then used silica gel column chromatography to strictly separate different CDs to obtain full-color fluorescence, and further elucidated that the differences in luminescence might be attributed to varying degrees of surface oxidation. In addition to the research progress reported above, many other studies induce the PL spectra red/blue shift or improve the PLQY value of phenylenediamine-based CDs by changing the reaction conditions [24–26], adjusting the molar ratio between the reactants [27,28], or selecting reaction solvents with different polarities [29–32]. Some of the strategies and methods are complex, but they have had a positive and far-reaching impact on promoting the luminescence regulation and PLQY improvement of CDs. However, the synergistic improvement of absolute PLQY value and wide spectral coverage is still a thorny issue in current research.

In 2023, Professor Gong's research group [33] discovered an unconventional luminescence behavior in phenylenediamine-based CDs during their study on luminescence regulation. They used a hydroxyl-rich 2,3-dihydroxynaphthalene molecule as the second precursor, and carried out different polymerization processes with oPD, mPD, and pPD molecules under the action of hydrochloric acid. The PL emission of the obtained CDs followed the rule of oPD, mPD, and pPD from blue to red, which differs from many previously reported results. Through an in-depth structural comparative analysis, they proposed that variations in the degree of polymerization, surface states, functional groups, and graphitic N content of the CDs might be the primary reasons for this unconventional luminescence behavior. This work utilized the same precursor to effectively change the polymerization processes and fluorescence emissions of the three phenylenediamine isomers, resulting in CDs with unconventional luminescence behavior. However, the introduction of strong acids such as hydrochloric acid will inevitably have an environmental impact during the post-treatment of the solution. Meanwhile, due to the lack of effective green fluorescent components and the narrow spectral coverage, high-quality white LEDs cannot be obtained. Therefore, we need to select an environmentally friendly acidic reagent to regulate the luminescence behavior of CDs to achieve a synergistic improvement in both high PLQY values and wide spectral coverage. Succinic acid (SA) is a low-cost, environmentally friendly weak acid reagent with a moderate carbon chain length. The carboxyl groups at both ends exhibit high reactivity, which is beneficial to promote the dehydrogenation and polymerization process of amino, hydroxyl, and other groups, regulate the polymerization structure and surface composition of CDs, and enable changes in energy level structure and optical properties. Based on this, we used structurally simple SA molecules to regulate the PL behavior of phenylenediamine-based CDs, resulting in DB-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs with high absolute PLQY values (68 %, 60 %, and 72 %) and wide spectral coverage, achieving high-quality white LEDs and live cell imaging. This method uses the same SA molecules and does not require changing the solvent ratio and reaction conditions, while achieving the regulation of the PL spectra and effective improvement of the absolute PLQY values. Through an in-depth analysis of the relationship between structure and PL properties, the formation process and reaction mechanism of CDs were revealed, and the reasons for their unconventional luminescence behavior were explained. This work can provide inspiration for the construction of CDs with high PLQY values and wide spectral coverage using phenylenediamine as the precursor.

oPD, mPD, pPD, and SA were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Anhydrous ethanol was purchased from Wuxi Yasheng Chemical Co., Ltd. (Wuxi, China). The reagents used in the experiments were all analytical grade without further purification.

Preparation of DB-CDs: Take 0.295 g of SA and 0.216 g of pPD (molar ratio of 1.25:1) and place them in a beaker containing 30 mL of anhydrous ethanol. Stir the mixture evenly and ultrasonicate for about 3 min. Then put them into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and react in an oven at 200 ℃ for 10 h. After the reaction is completed, cool naturally to room temperature. The crude product was purified by rigorous silica gel column chromatography using ethanol as the eluent. The obtained CD solution emitted a bright, deep blue fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet light irradiation. Finally, CD powder was obtained by rotary evaporation and vacuum drying at 60 ℃, with a yield of about 41 %.

Preparation of G-CDs: Take 0.295 g of SA and 0.216 g of mPD (molar ratio of 1.25:1) and place them in a beaker containing 30 mL of anhydrous ethanol. Stir the mixture evenly and ultrasonicate for about 3 min. Then put them into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and react in an oven at 200 ℃ for 10 h. After the reaction is completed, cool naturally to room temperature. The crude product was purified by rigorous silica gel column chromatography using ethanol as the eluent. The obtained CD solution emitted a bright, green fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet light irradiation. Finally, CD powder was obtained by rotary evaporation and vacuum drying at 60 ℃, with a yield of about 38 %.

Preparation of R-CDs: Take 0.295 g of SA and 0.216 g of oPD (molar ratio of 1.25:1) and place them in a beaker containing 30 mL of anhydrous ethanol. Stir the mixture evenly and ultrasonicate for about 3 min. Then put them into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and react in an oven at 200 ℃ for 10 h. After the reaction is completed, cool naturally to room temperature. The crude product was purified by rigorous silica gel column chromatography using ethanol as the eluent. The obtained CD solution emitted a bright, red fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet light irradiation. Finally, CD powder was obtained by rotary evaporation and vacuum drying at 60 ℃, with a yield of about 22 %.

Control Experiments: The following are the control experiments without adding SA. Weigh 0.216 g of mPD and place it in a beaker containing 30 mL of anhydrous ethanol. Stir evenly and ultrasonicate for about 3 min. Then put them into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and react in an oven at 200 ℃ for 10 h. After the reaction is completed, cool naturally to room temperature. The crude product was purified by rigorous silica gel column chromatography using ethanol as the eluent. The obtained CD solution emitted blue fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet light irradiation. Finally, CD powder was obtained by rotary evaporation and vacuum drying at 60 ℃, with a yield of about 34 %. In addition, the preparation method and related structural characterization of yellow CDs obtained by solvothermal reaction of single oPD were recorded in detail in our previously published work [21]. Similarly, the preparation method and related structural characterization of red CDs obtained from the solvothermal reaction of pPD alone were also detailed in our previously published work [34].

Fabrication of DB-LED: Take 0.01 g of DB-CDs and dissolve them in 1.5 mL of anhydrous ethanol solution. Then, 0.25 g of PVP was added and sonicated for 3 min. After it is evenly dissolved, use a 20 μL pipette to take out the mixed solution and drop it into the LED lamp cap, and place it in an oven at 50 ℃ for 3 h. Finally, it is encapsulated on an LED chip with a wavelength of 380 nm and waits for testing.
Fabrication of G-LED: The entire fabrication process is the same as that of the DB-LED. The only change is to replace 0.01 g of DB-CDs with 0.01 g of G-CDs.
Fabrication of R-LED: The entire fabrication process is the same as that of the DB-LED. The only change is to replace 0.01 g of DB-CDs with 0.02 g of R-CDs.

Fabrication of WLED: Take 0.01 g, 0.01 g, and 0.03 g of DB-, G-, and R-CDs, respectively, mixed and dissolved in 1.5 mL of anhydrous ethanol solution. Then 0.25 g PVP was added and sonicated for 3 min. Subsequently, use a 20 μL pipette to take out the mixed solution and drop it into the lamp cap of the LED, and place it in the oven at 50 ℃ for 3 h. Finally, it is encapsulated on an LED chip with a wavelength of 380 nm and waits for testing.
HepG2 cells were grown at 37 ℃ with 5 % CO2 in an incubator after being cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 100 U/mL streptomycin-penicillin.

The cytotoxic effect of R-CDs on HeLa cells was assessed using Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK8) assays. Cells (8 × 103 cells/well) were seeded in 96-well plates and incubated. After adhering for 24 h, the cells were treated with different concentrations (0.0, 20.0, 40.0, 60.0, 80.0, and 100.0 μg/mL) of R-CDs for an additional 24 h. After removing the supernatant, 100 μL of culture medium and 10 μL of CCK8 solution were added to each well, protected from light. The absorbance at 450 nm was measured using a microplate reader after 2.5 h of incubation.

HepG2 cells were seeded into a 12-well cell culture dish (8 × 104 cells/well) containing DMEM supplemented with 10 % FBS and incubated at 37 ℃ in 5 % CO2 for 24 h. Wash the cells three times with PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4) to remove non-adherent cells. Then, incubate the cells in DMEM containing 40 μg/mL R-CDs for 60 min. The HepG2 cells were washed three times with PBS before imaging. Finally, the fluorescence images of the cells were observed using a Zeiss LSM880 confocal laser scanning microscope.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained by the Thermo Scientific Talos F200X microscope. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recognized with the Thermo Fisher Scientific Nicolet 5700 spectrometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using the Panalytical Empyrean X-ray diffractometer. Raman spectra were gathered using a Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution spectrometer at an excitation wavelength of 532 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha instrument. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were conducted by the Shimadzu RF-6000 fluorescence spectrometer. The absolute PLQY values and time-resolved PL decay curves were gathered by the Horiba Jobin Yvon PluoroLog 3-TCSPC steady/transient-state fluorescence spectrometer equipped with an integrating sphere. The photoelectric properties of the multicolor LEDs and WLEDs were measured by the Photo Research PR-655 SpectraScan. Fluorescence images of HepG2 cells were captured using a Zeiss LSM880 confocal laser scanning microscope.

As shown in Scheme 1 , we selected SA molecules with four carbon chain lengths and carboxyl functional groups at both ends. These molecules were crosslinked and polymerized with pPD, mPD, and oPD, respectively, to obtain CDs with bright deep blue, green, and red fluorescence. This is in complete contrast to most previously reported results (the fluorescence emission from blue to red follows the order of mPD, oPD, and pPD). To further investigate this unconventional luminescence behavior, the optical properties of the CDs are compared and discussed in detail. Fig. 1 (a–c) shows the 3D normalized UV–vis absorption, Ex, and Em spectra of DB-, G-, and R-CDs. The absorption peaks in the 200–300 nm region can be attributed to the π–π* transitions of C=C bonds. Specifically, the signal bands of DB-CDs are located at 205 and 245 nm; G-CDs exhibit absorption peaks at 235 and 293 nm; and the characteristic signals of R-CDs are found at 215, 243, and 273 nm. In addition, obvious absorption signals were also observed in the low-energy region (300–600 nm), which was mainly attributed to the n–π* transition of C=O/C=N bonds. The absorption peaks for DB-, G-, and R-CDs are located at 303 nm, 460 nm, and at 536 and 575 nm, respectively. This indicates the presence of different types of surface state transitions in the CDs, suggesting they possess distinct surface states. The characteristic absorption peak of R-CDs can be extended to the long wavelength region (500–600 nm), indicating that it has a certain conjugated structure [5,35–37]. The inset is the digital photographs of the CD solution irradiated by sunlight and UV light, showing bright deep blue, green, and red fluorescence, respectively. It is worth noting that the overlap between the UV–vis absorption and the Em spectra is very small (Fig. S1, Supporting Information), which can effectively avoid self-absorption and is conducive to achieving stronger fluorescence emission [38]. Fig. 1(d–f) shows the PL spectra. The emission peaks of DB-, G-, and R-CDs are fixed at 440 nm, 510 nm, and 600, 650, 710 nm, respectively, showing obvious excitation-independent fluorescence characteristics. This means the optical properties of CDs do not change with the excitation wavelength, resulting in relatively stable fluorescence regions (Fig. S2, Supporting Information). Fig. 1(g–i) shows the 3D excitation-emission matrix (EEM) spectra, the optimal excitation wavelengths of DB-, G-, and R-CDs are 370, 440, and 560 nm, respectively, which are consistent with the peak positions of the Ex curves in Fig. 1(a–c). We further normalized the emission spectra and found that the PL spectra overlap, covering the entire visible region from 400 to 850 nm (Fig. 2 (a)). This also confirms the potential application of CDs in full-color/white light display devices. According to the calculations from the color coordinate program, the CIE color coordinates of DB-, G-, and R-CDs are (0.15, 0.09), (0.27, 0.60), and (0.65, 0.35), respectively, which belong to the categories of deep blue, green, and red emission (Fig. 2(b)). The color coordinate value of DB-CDs is very close to the standard color Rec. 2020 (0.131, 0.046) specification [39], confirming its excellent luminescent performance. We also used a steady/transient-state fluorescence spectrometer equipped with an integrating sphere to investigate the absolute PLQY values and time-resolved PL decay curves of the CDs. At the optimal excitation wavelengths, the absolute PLQY values of DB-, G-, and R-CDs were 68 %, 60 %, and 72 %, respectively (Figs. S3–S5, Supporting Information). Compared with the single phenylenediamine reaction system, the absolute PLQY values have been significantly improved after adding SA molecules, from less than 30 % to more than 60 % (Fig. 2(c)). In addition, the emission spectra have also changed significantly. We use the same small organic acid to regulate the phenylenediamine isomer and obtain CDs with fluorescence in different shift directions, expanding the overlap and coverage of the spectra. We also compared the PLQY values of published phenylenediamine-derived CDs literature and found that the absolute PLQY values of obtained three primary color CDs are all at a relatively high level (Table S1, Supporting Information), further confirming the excellent optical properties of CDs and the novelty of the design strategy. The amidation process of SA molecules introduces additional electron-withdrawing groups, such as carboxyl and C=O functional groups. These functional groups modify the surface of CDs and change the surface structure and energy level, which is beneficial in reducing non-radiative recombination centers. Through the synergistic interaction with electron-donating groups like amino groups, the PLQY value of CDs is significantly enhanced, and the PL spectrum is prone to a redshift [40–43], effectively achieving a synergistic improvement in high PLQY values and wide spectral coverage. The time-resolved PL decay curves of DB-, G-, and R-CDs can be well fitted by a single exponential function (Fig. 2(d–f)), with average lifetimes of 8.26, 4.34, and 2.31 ns, respectively. The nanosecond-scale lifetimes prove that CDs have fluorescence characteristics. In addition, the different lifetimes mean that three CDs undergo different luminescence processes, and their lifetime gradually decreases with the red shift of the PL spectra, which is consistent with the previously reported results [23,35,42]. More detailed parameters are recorded in Table S2 in Supporting Information. Furthermore, we use the Tauc plot method to calculate the band gaps according to the formula (αhν)n = A(hν-Eg). Here, α is the absorption coefficient, hν is the photon energy, A is a constant, and Eg is the band gap. When n = 2, the direct band gap of CDs can be obtained by extrapolating the linear region of these curves to the abscissa [36,44,45]. The band gap values of DB-, G-, and R-CDs are calculated to be 3.75, 2.55, and 2.08 eV (Fig. 2(g)). Subsequently, we present the variation of the band gaps for three CDs. It can be clearly observed that the band gaps of CDs gradually decreased with the redshift of the emission spectra (Fig. 2(h)).

To further investigate the influencing factors of PL spectra redshift, we studied the microstructure and composition of DB-, G-, and R-CDs. From the TEM images, it can be observed that the CDs exhibit a quasi-spherical structure, with clear morphology and uniform dispersion. The particle size distribution of DB-CDs ranges from 1.0 to 2.4 nm, with an average particle size of 1.6 nm. The particle size distributions of G-CDs and R-CDs range from 3.0 to 6.0 nm and 5.0 to 7.5 nm, with average particle sizes of 4.3 and 6.1 nm, respectively (Fig. 3 (a–c)). The insets are HRTEM images showing well-resolved lattice fringes with uniform spacing. The lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponds to the (100) plane of graphite, indicating that the CDs have a certain degree of crystallinity [46]. The narrow particle size distribution also suggests that the CDs have good size uniformity. Additionally, as the particle size of CDs increases, the emission peak gradually moves to longer wavelengths (Fig. 3(d)), indicating that the redshift of the PL spectra may be related to the quantum size effect [47,48]. To obtain the surface group content, crystallization characteristics, graphitization degree, elemental composition, and bonding types of CDs, FTIR, XRD, Raman, and XPS characterizations were performed. In the FTIR spectra (Fig. 3(e)), the broad signal peaks observed in the range of 3245–3648 cm−1 correspond to the –OH and –NH2 groups, indicating that CDs have a certain water solubility. The absorption peaks at 2980 and 2930 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of the C–H bond. The characteristic signal at 1727 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the C=O bond in the carboxyl functional group, indicating that the SA molecules participate in the formation of CDs, and the enhancement of the absorption peak suggests that the oxidation degree of CDs gradually increases. At 1630 cm−1, the characteristic peak corresponds to the stretching vibration of the C=O/C=N bonds, and the signal peak at 1450 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the C–N bond, which further indicates the existence of the amide functional group, suggesting that the carboxyl group of SA molecules and the amino group of the phenylenediamine isomer molecules underwent dehydration condensation, resulting in effective cross-linking. The absorption peak at 1423 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the C=C bond, and the enhancement of the signal indicates that R-CDs may have a relatively large sp2-conjugated carbon structure. The signals at 1170 and 1050 cm−1 correspond to the bending vibrations of C–O and C–H bonds, respectively [23,24,26,33,36,49]. In the XRD diffraction patterns (Fig. 3(f)), CDs exhibit a broad diffraction peak at about 22°, which can be attributed to the (002) plane of the graphite structure. This typical diffraction peak has also been reported in previous work [42,50–53]. In the Raman spectra, 1360 cm−1 corresponds to the disordered band (D-band) of carbon materials, and 1570 cm−1 corresponds to the graphite band (G-band) of carbon materials. The intensity ratios of G-band and D-band (IG/ID) of DB-, G-, and R-CDs are 1.05, 1.03, and 1.09, respectively (Fig. 3(g)), indicating that the CDs have a certain degree of graphitization [54], which is consistent with the obvious lattice fringes observed in HRTEM images. In the full-scan XPS spectra, CDs contain three elements: C, N, and O, which are located at binding energies of 285, 400, and 532 eV, respectively. Due to the relatively low nitrogen content in R-CDs, we enlarged the characterization image to clearly see the signal of the N element (Fig. 3(h)). From DB-CDs to R-CDs, the C content increases slightly, and the O content also gradually increases (Fig. 3(i)), indicating that the red shift of the PL spectra may be related to the increased degree of oxidation. This result is consistent with the analysis of the FTIR spectra. The data on the relative contents of C, N, and O are recorded in Table S3 of the Supporting Information. We further compared the high-resolution C1s, N1s, and O1s spectra and found that the bonding types of CDs are the same, but the relative contents are quite different. The high-resolution C1s spectra of CDs can be deconvoluted into four components: C–C/C=C, C–N, C–O, and C=O [55,56]. The positions of different binding energy peaks have been recorded in detail in Fig. 4 (a–c). During the change from DB-CDs to R-CDs, the content of C–C/C=C gradually increased from 41.51 % to 46.25 %, and the content of C=O functional groups also increased from 12.07 % to 18.03 % (Fig. 4(d) and Table S4). Meanwhile, the overall content of carbon and oxygen also shows an increasing trend, rising from 77.59 % and 9.56 % to 80.87 % and 18.31 %, respectively (Table S3, Supporting Information). From the above comparison results, it can be concluded that with the red shift of the PL spectra, the contents of sp2-conjugated carbon and C=O groups of CDs are significantly increased. High-resolution N1s spectra show characteristic signals of three types of nitrogen: pyridine N, amino N, and graphite N (Fig. 4(e–g)). The binding energy peaks for DB-CDs are recorded at 398.52, 399.11, and 400.17 eV. For G-CDs, these peaks appear at 398.73, 399.32, and 400.10 eV. Meanwhile, R-CDs exhibit signal peaks at 398.12, 399.51, and 400.71 eV [24,57]. Based on the variation in nitrogen content of different types, it can be observed that with the redshift in PL spectra, the content of pyridinic N decreases, while the content of graphitic N increases (Fig. 4(h) and Table S5). However, due to the reduction in overall nitrogen content (see Table S3 in the Supporting Information), the levels of all types of nitrogen have decreased. Additionally, the high-resolution O1s spectra can be deconvoluted into C=O and C–O groups (Fig. 4(i–k)). For DB-CDs, the signal peaks are observed at 531.76 and 533.04 eV. In the case of G-CDs, the peaks are located at 531.68 and 532.05 eV. Meanwhile, R-CDs exhibit signal peaks at 531.43 and 533.18 eV [58,59]. By comparing the changes in the content of two functional groups, it can be observed that as the fluorescence emission shifts from deep blue to red, the content of the C=O groups gradually increased from 53.06 % to 79.56 %. Concurrently, the total oxygen content rised from 9.56 % to 18.31 % (Fig. 4(l), Table S3 and S5), which results in R-CDs having the highest C=O functional group content. From the above discussion, it can be concluded that the fluorescence emission of CDs will be affected by the quantum size effect. Additionally, with the increase of sp2-conjugated domains and C=O functional group content, the PL spectra gradually red-shifted, and the PLQY value gradually increased.

So why do DB-, G-, and R-CDs exhibit unconventional luminescent behavior? To investigate this question, we conducted control experiments without adding SA molecules (the preparation method is detailed in the experimental section). Firstly, the amino functional groups of a single pPD molecule are located at opposite positions, resulting in relatively weak interactions and steric hindrance effects. Therefore, the pPD molecules may exhibit higher reactivity during the solvothermal process. In the initial stage of the reaction, pPD molecules will self-polymerize in the ethanol solution and gradually condense to form long-chain polymers. With the extension of reaction time, the long-chain polymers are cross-linked through the dehydrogenation process, and in situ carbonization occurs, forming red CDs with a large sp2-conjugated domain (fluorescence emission peak at 610 nm). This results were confirmed in our previously published research work [34]. Different from the formation process of red CDs, when SA molecules are introduced into the reaction system, dehydration condensation easily occurs to form amide functional groups due to the high reactivity between the carboxyl and amino groups (Fig. 5 (a)). In this way, the original long-chain polymerization process of the pPD molecule will inevitably be destroyed, resulting in a corresponding reduction in the sp2-conjugated domain of the formed CDs. We compared the bonding content of DB-CDs with that of the red CDs obtained with single pPD molecules, which also proved this conclusion: in the full-scan XPS spectra, the total carbon content decreased from 80.30 % to 77.59 %. At the same time, in the high-resolution C1s spectra, the C–C/C=C content decreased from 44.25 % to 41.51 % [34], proving that the conjugated carbon content of CDs has significantly reduced. This indicates that under the action of SA molecules, the amidation reaction disrupted the original polymerization process of the pPD molecules, induced structural changes, and destroyed the conjugated domains of the carbon core, causing a blue shift in fluorescence emission. Secondly, the single mPD molecules can easily form blue CDs after the self-polymerization and carbonization process. The microstructure of these CDs exhibits a quasi-spherical structure with uniform dispersion and obvious lattice fringes. Their average particle size is 2.4 nm, with a narrow distribution (Fig. S6(a)(b), Supporting Information). The fluorescence emission peak of the blue CDs is located at 430 nm, which represents an 80 nm shift compared to G-CDs (see Fig. S7(b)(c) in the Supporting Information). The –OH, –NH2, C–H, C=N/C=O, and C=C groups were found in the FTIR spectra, but there was no amide functional group (Fig. S7(d), Supporting Information), which is in contrast to the characteristic amide signals shown in the FTIR spectra of G-CDs. Similarly, the XRD diffraction pattern shows a broad diffraction peak around 22°, corresponding to the (002) plane of the graphite structure (Fig. S7(e) in the Supporting Information). In addition, we compared the bonding contents between G-CDs and the blue CDs formed by mPD molecules alone. The high-resolution C1s spectra show a significant increase in the content of C–C/C=C from blue CDs to G-CDs, rising from 33.98 % to 43.69 %. Similarly, the high-resolution O1s spectra reveal an obvious increase in the content of C=O, from 16.93 % to 63.31 % (Fig. S7(f–i) and Tables S6–S8 in the Supporting Information). This indicates that under the action of SA molecules, the CDs surface will bond more C=O groups due to the amidation reaction, resulting in a change in the surface structure. After further polymerization and in-situ carbonization, G-CDs are formed (Fig. 5(b)), achieving the regulation of fluorescence from blue to green. The last is the reaction system of oPD molecules. Unlike the previous reaction process, single oPD molecules easy to form DAP molecules at high temperatures. As the reaction time increased, dehydrogenation occurred between DAP molecules to form conjugated aromatic polymers. With the intensification of the polymerization and carbonization process, a large amount of yellow CDs was gradually formed (emission peak of about 550 nm), which was also confirmed in our previously published work [21]. Unlike the formation process of yellow CDs, when SA molecules are introduced into the reaction system, the carboxyl groups of SA molecules will dehydration condensation with the amino groups of oPD and DAP molecules to form amide bonds. The amide signal in the FTIR spectra also confirms this result (Fig. 3(e)). With the intensification of the amidation reaction, cross-linking, and in situ carbonization processes, R-CDs with a conjugated structure and a large amount of cross-linked C=O groups were obtained (Fig. 5(c)). In addition, we compared the bonding content of R-CDs with that of yellow CDs obtained by oPD molecules alone, which further confirmed this conclusion: in the full-scan XPS spectra, the total oxygen content increased from 16.52 % to 18.31 %, and in the high-resolution O1s spectra, the content of C=O groups risen from 50.19 % to 79.56 % [21]. This indicates that under the action of SA molecules, more C=O and –COOH functional groups will be cross-linked on the surface of CDs, realizing the conversion from yellow to red fluorescence. Through the comparison and analysis of the above composition structures, the introduction of SA molecules can significantly regulate the structure and PL properties of phenylenediamine-based CDs. This effectively achieves the conversion of pPD-based CDs from red to deep blue emission, mPD-based CDs from blue to green emission, and oPD-based CDs from yellow to red emission. As a result, DB-, G-, and R-CDs with unconventional luminescence behaviors were obtained.

Since DB-, G-, and R-CDs have high PLQY values (68 %, 60 %, and 72 %), and their PL spectra overlap and cover the entire visible light region from 400 to 850 nm, we explored their potential applications in the fields of lighting and displays. First, different concentrations of CDs were compounded with PVP to prepare fluorescent conversion materials (the fabrication method is detailed in the experimental section). The fluorescent conversion materials were then cured and encapsulated into LED devices. After connecting the positive and negative electrodes of the chip base, the power was turned on for testing. In Fig. 6 (a–d), the LED devices emit bright deep blue, green, red, and white fluorescence, respectively. Under the irradiation of multicolor LEDs and WLEDs, the circular filter paper and the school emblem with the Chinese characters “Southeast University” both exhibit the corresponding luminous colors of the devices. Especially under WLEDs irradiation, the original white color of the circular filter paper, the yellow background of the school emblem, and the details of the Chinese characters “Southeast University” are clearly visible. This effectively shows the actual colors and details of the objects, further demonstrating the potential application of the obtained CDs in lighting and display devices. To investigate the optoelectronic properties of the devices, the Photo Research PR-655 spectrometer was used to test the optical performance of multicolor LEDs and WLEDs. In Fig. 6(e) and (i), under the excitation of the UV chip, the emission spectrum of the DB-LED is mainly concentrated in the range of 410–480 nm, and the CIE color coordinates are (0.16, 0.10), which belongs to the deep blue emission region. The G-LED exhibits an emission spectrum primarily focused in the range of 470–550 nm, with CIE color coordinates of (0.25, 0.40), which belongs to the green emission region (Fig. 6(f) and (j)). The R-LED's emission spectrum is primarily within the 580–720 nm range, and with CIE color coordinates of (0.52, 0.29), it falls into the red emission region (Fig. 6(g) and (k)). Due to the mixture of DB-, G-, and R-CDs, the WLED device exhibits a broad emission peak that covers the entire visible spectrum. The device has CIE coordinates of (0.34, 0.33), which are very close to the pure white light coordinates of (0.33, 0.33), with a CCT of 4768 K, CRI of 82, and a luminous efficiency of 18.98 lm/W. Pure white light can clearly show the actual color and details of the object, the CCT of 4768 K is suitable for indoor lighting, demonstrating significant potential and value (Fig. 6(h) and (l)). Table S9 also lists the parameter comparison of previously reported WLEDs, highlighting the advantages of this work. In addition, we tested the emission spectra of multicolor LEDs and WLEDs at various voltages (Fig. S8(a–d) in Supporting Information). With the continuous increase of the driving voltage, the luminous intensity of the LED devices also gradually increased. There was no shift or attenuation in the peak position, indicating that the CDs-based fluorescence conversion material cured and encapsulated in the device did not show a saturation phenomenon under the irradiation of the UV chip, which also confirmed that the prepared multicolor LEDs and WLEDs have a certain stability.

Furthermore, the obtained R-CDs exhibit a high absolute PLQY value of up to 72 %, and their fluorescence emission wavelengths of 600, 650 and 710 nm allow for deeper tissue penetration, effectively minimizing interference from the organism's intrinsic fluorescence. Therefore, we further explored the potential application of R-CDs in bioimaging. First, the CCK8 assay was used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of R-CDs in HepG2 cells [60]. As shown in Fig. S9, after incubating HepG2 cells with R-CDs for 24 h, cell viability remained above 98 % even at R-CD concentrations as high as 100 µg/mL. This indicates that R-CDs have good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. Subsequently, we used R-CDs as fluorescent imaging agents to investigate their imaging capabilities in HepG2 cells. The first line of images in Fig. 7 shows the control group experiment. The morphology of the live cells can be observed in the bright field images, while under confocal laser excitation, the images appear entirely black, effectively eliminating interference from other fluorescence sources. Additionally, HepG2 cells incubated with R-CDs emitted bright red fluorescence under confocal laser excitation (second line in Fig. 7). The distinct spindle-shaped morphology indicates the active state of the HepG2 cells. The fluorescence images perfectly overlap with the bright field images, confirming that the R-CDs can penetrate the cell membrane and label HepG2 cells without damaging them. This further confirms the potential of R-CDs as fluorescent probes for live cell imaging, offering significant advantages for applications in the biomedical field.

In summary, we designed a luminescence regulation method for phenylenediamine-based CDs. By selecting environmentally friendly, structurally simple, and cost-effective SA molecules, and utilizing the amidation process to effectively alter the polymerization structure of the original phenylenediamine molecules, we obtained DB-, G-, and R-CDs with unconventional luminescent behaviors and high PLQY values (68 %, 60 %, and 72 %). Their PL spectra overlap and cover the entire visible light region from 400 to 850 nm, which achieved the synergistic enhancement of high PLQY value and wide spectral coverage. By comparing the morphology and structure, we found that the PL redshift between the CDs is primarily determined by the sp2-conjugated domains of the carbon core and the content of surface C=O functional groups. Based on this, we proposed the formation process and reaction mechanism for DB-, G-, and R-CDs. In addition, by comparing the composition and structure of CDs formed by phenylenediamine molecules alone, it was found that this unconventional luminescence behavior was mainly affected by the content of conjugated domains and surface cross-linked C=O and –COOH functional groups. Finally, multicolor LEDs and WLEDs were prepared using mixtures of CDs and PVP at different concentrations. The WLED device exhibits CIE coordinates of (0.34, 0.33), which are very close to the pure white light coordinates of (0.33, 0.33), with a CCT of 4768 K and a CRI of 82. Meanwhile, the R-CDs, which have a high absolute PLQY value (72 %) and low biological toxicity, were used in live cell imaging. This work not only effectively achieved the preparation of high PLQY tricolor CDs but also provided a new idea for unconventional luminescence regulation with wide spectral coverage.
